finding.summary: The acct_wrapper module forces access-control bits to 1 via we_flag regardless of privilege level or lock state, allowing a write-flag bypass to unconditionally grant permissions for peripheral index 0 to all access levels.

evidence.description: Line 49: `assign acc_ctrl_o = {acct_mem[3*0+2], acct_mem[3*0+1], acct_mem[3*0+0]|{8{we_flag}}};` The lowest 8 bits of acc_ctrl_o (slave 0 access control) are unconditionally OR-ed with 8 copies of we_flag. This means any asserting of we_flag forces all 8 access-control bits for slave 0 to 1, bypassing whatever values are stored in acct_mem and bypassing any reglk_ctrl write-lock protection.
evidence.supports_claim: we_flag can unconditionally override access control output for slave 0, bypassing stored permissions and register-lock protection

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_c is derived from acc_ctrl (which comes from acc_ctrl_o of acct_wrapper) via bit-reorganization at line 222. The corrupted acc_ctrl_o is then distributed as acct_ctrl_i to hmac_wrapper, pkt_wrapper, reglk_wrapper, and acct_wrapper itself, meaning the we_flag override propagates to gating of all downstream peripheral enable signals.
evidence.supports_claim: Corrupted access-control value flows to all peripherals that gate their enable on acct_ctrl_i

evidence.description: In riscv_peripherals.sv the acct_wrapper instance receives we_flag_0 as we_flag input without any privilege-level guard, confirming the path from we_flag to acc_ctrl_o is unguarded at the instantiation level.
evidence.supports_claim: No privilege check is applied to we_flag before it reaches the acc_ctrl_o override logic

evidence.description: Write side (lines 87-111) checks reglk_ctrl bits before updating acct_mem, and enable requires both en (gated by acct_ctrl_i) and we. However, the acc_ctrl_o output at line 49 bypasses all of these guards by ORing we_flag directly into the output, so the stored value in acct_mem is irrelevant when we_flag is asserted.
evidence.supports_claim: Existing reglk_ctrl write-lock guards on acct_mem are bypassed because acc_ctrl_o is constructed outside those guards

evidence.description: hmac_wrapper gates its enable as `assign en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i`. If acct_ctrl_i is forced to 1 by the we_flag bypass, unprivileged software can gain read/write access to HMAC registers regardless of the intended access control policy.
evidence.supports_claim: Forced acct_ctrl_i=1 grants unauthorized access to security-critical HMAC peripheral

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper similarly gates enable via `assign en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i`. A forced acct_ctrl_i=1 would allow unauthorized writes to reglk_ctrl_o, which controls register-lock bits for all peripherals, enabling a full security bypass.
evidence.supports_claim: Unauthorized access to reglk_wrapper allows disabling all register locks across the system

evidence.description: The commented-out line 516 `//assign rom_req = rom_req_acct;` and the active line 517 `assign rom_req = rom_req_acct && acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][0]` shows boot ROM access is gated on acc_ctrl_c bit 0 of peripheral 0. If we_flag forces this bit to 1, boot ROM becomes accessible to any privilege level.
evidence.supports_claim: Boot ROM access control is defeated by the we_flag override of acc_ctrl_o bit[0]

finding.reasoning_summary: The acct_wrapper computes acc_ctrl_o by OR-ing 8 copies of the we_flag input into the lowest 8 bits of the output (line 49), completely bypassing both the stored acct_mem values and the reglk_ctrl write-lock protection for slave 0's access control. This corrupted value propagates through riscv_peripherals as acc_ctrl_c and is distributed to hmac_wrapper, pkt_wrapper, reglk_wrapper, and the boot ROM access check. Asserting we_flag forces acct_ctrl_i=1 for peripheral 0 at all privilege levels, granting unauthorized enable to all peripherals that depend on this signal, including the security-critical HMAC and register-lock controller.
finding.security_impact: An attacker able to assert we_flag (e.g., via a write transaction that sets the flag) can force acc_ctrl_o[7:0] to 0xFF, granting full access to peripheral 0 at all privilege levels. Since reglk_wrapper and hmac_wrapper receive this as their acct_ctrl_i enable gate, the attacker gains unauthorized access to register-lock controls (allowing disabling of all peripheral locks) and HMAC key material. Boot ROM access is also unconditionally enabled. This represents a complete bypass of the hardware access-control enforcement layer.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The precise conditions under which we_flag_0 is asserted in riscv_peripherals are not shown in the provided snippets (only the signal declaration at line 1718 is visible). The exact threat model for we_flag (whether it is software-writable or hardware-only) would further refine exploitability, but the structural bypass is confirmed by the source code evidence alone.

finding.summary: The acct_wrapper reset logic initializes acct_mem to all 0xFFFFFFFF (fully permissive) on reset, and the acc_ctrl_o output unconditionally ORs we_flag into the lowest access-control word, allowing external we_flag input to force permission bits open regardless of the stored access-control values.

evidence.description: On reset (~rst_ni || rst_6), all acct_mem entries are initialized to 32'hffffffff. This means every access-control permission bit is set to 1 (fully open) at reset, rather than a safe locked-down default.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset initializes the access-control memory to a fully permissive state, which is an unsafe reset default for a permission-controlling peripheral.

evidence.description: Line 49: assign acc_ctrl_o = {acct_mem[3*0+2], acct_mem[3*0+1], acct_mem[3*0+0]|{8{we_flag}}}; The we_flag input is unconditionally OR'd into the lowest 8 bits of the first access-control entry. Any external agent that can assert we_flag can force those permission bits to 1, bypassing whatever value is stored in acct_mem[0] and any reglk lock that would otherwise prevent modification.
evidence.supports_claim: we_flag bypasses the register lock (reglk_ctrl) and the stored acct_mem value to force permission bits open, violating the access-control protection model.

evidence.description: Write-side guard: acct_mem[00] is protected by reglk_ctrl[5]. Read-side: entries are zeroed when corresponding reglk bits are set. However, the acc_ctrl_o combinational output at line 49 bypasses both of these guards via we_flag.
evidence.supports_claim: The reglk guards on the write and read paths do not protect the acc_ctrl_o output path, which is driven directly by a combinational expression including we_flag.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_c is derived from acc_ctrl (which comes from acc_ctrl_o of acct_wrapper) and is distributed to hmac_wrapper, pkt_wrapper, reglk_wrapper, and acct_wrapper itself as acct_ctrl_i, gating access to all peripherals. If we_flag forces acc_ctrl_o bits high, all downstream peripheral access controls are unlocked.
evidence.supports_claim: The corrupted acc_ctrl_o value propagates system-wide to all peripheral access-control inputs, amplifying the impact of the we_flag bypass.

evidence.description: riscv_peripherals instantiates acct_wrapper and connects acc_ctrl_o back to acc_ctrl, which is then reshaped into acc_ctrl_c and distributed to all peripheral wrappers as acct_ctrl_i.
evidence.supports_claim: Confirms the propagation path from acct_wrapper's acc_ctrl_o to all downstream peripherals.

finding.reasoning_summary: Two concrete vulnerabilities are present. First, on any reset event, acct_mem is set to all-ones (0xFFFFFFFF), meaning all access-control permissions are open immediately after reset rather than being locked down. Second, the combinational output acc_ctrl_o permanently OR's the external we_flag signal into acct_mem[0]'s lowest byte, meaning any agent that can assert we_flag can unconditionally force the first access-control entry's low 8 bits to 1, bypassing the reglk lock and the stored memory value. Since acc_ctrl_o feeds into acc_ctrl_c which gates access to all peripherals (hmac, pkt, reglk, acct), this bypass has system-wide impact.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can control we_flag (an external input to acct_wrapper) can force peripheral access-control bits open, granting access to otherwise locked peripherals including HMAC key material (hmac_wrapper), packet key storage (pkt_wrapper), and the register lock controller (reglk_wrapper). Additionally, the unsafe reset default means that immediately after reset and before software reprograms the ACL table, all peripherals are fully accessible to all privilege levels.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source of we_flag and whether it is attacker-controllable is not shown in the provided snippets; however the structural bypass via OR is unconditional in hardware. The exact reset semantics of rst_6 sourcing is shown but the triggering conditions are not fully elaborated.

finding.summary: The acct_wrapper reset logic initializes acct_mem to 0xFFFFFFFF (all-permissive) on reset, and the read-lock bits used differ from the write-lock bits, creating an asymmetry where different reglk_ctrl bits guard reads versus writes for the same memory entries. Additionally, the acc_ctrl_o output permanently ORs in the we_flag signal to the lowest access-control entry, allowing a hardware flag to bypass the stored access-control values.

evidence.description: On reset (lines 81-85), acct_mem is initialized to 32'hffffffff for all entries. This means the access-control table defaults to fully permissive (all bits set), so any reset event leaves all permissions open until explicitly locked down by software.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset initializes access-control memory to all-ones (fully permissive), creating an unsafe default state.

evidence.description: Write guard uses reglk_ctrl[5] for entries 0-2 and reglk_ctrl[13] for entries 3-5, while read guard uses reglk_ctrl[4] for entries 0-2 and reglk_ctrl[2] for entries 3-5 (lines 90-139). The lock-bit indices differ between read and write paths for the same memory entries, meaning a lock bit that blocks reads does not necessarily block writes and vice versa.
evidence.supports_claim: Asymmetric lock-bit assignment between read and write paths for the same acct_mem entries allows one direction to remain unprotected when the other is locked.

evidence.description: Line 49: acc_ctrl_o = {acct_mem[3*0+2], acct_mem[3*0+1], acct_mem[3*0+0]|{8{we_flag}}}. The we_flag input is unconditionally OR'd into the lowest 8 bits of the access-control output regardless of the reglk_ctrl lock state. This allows the we_flag hardware signal to grant access-control permissions that override whatever is stored in acct_mem[0], bypassing the register-lock protection entirely for that entry.
evidence.supports_claim: we_flag can force access-control bits high in acc_ctrl_o without any lock-check, bypassing the reglk_ctrl protection for the first slave's access-control entry.

evidence.description: we_flag_0 from riscv_peripherals is connected to acct_wrapper's we_flag input (line 1718 area). This is an externally-driven signal fed directly into the access-control output computation.
evidence.supports_claim: The we_flag signal originates outside acct_wrapper and is not gated by any permission check before influencing acc_ctrl_o.

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper drives reglk_ctrl_o which is fed back as reglk_ctrl to all peripheral wrappers including acct_wrapper. The acct_wrapper itself also receives reglk_ctrl_i[6*8+7:6*8] as its own lock slice, meaning the ACCT module's own lock state is managed externally and the module simply passes it through (assign reglk_ctrl = reglk_ctrl_i at line 51).
evidence.supports_claim: The lock control is externally managed and passed through without local enforcement beyond the register checks shown.

finding.reasoning_summary: Three concrete issues are evidenced: (1) Reset sets acct_mem to 0xFFFFFFFF making all access controls permissive by default; (2) Different reglk_ctrl bit indices are used for read guards versus write guards on the same memory entries, creating an asymmetric protection scheme where locking reads does not lock writes and vice versa; (3) The acc_ctrl_o output unconditionally OR's the external we_flag signal into the first slave's access-control bits, allowing hardware to force permissions open without any reglk_ctrl check, bypassing the register-lock mechanism for that entry.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can assert we_flag (or trigger a reset) can override access-control values that are supposed to be locked by reglk_ctrl. The all-permissive reset default means a reset attack opens all access controls. The read/write lock asymmetry means a partial lock configuration may leave one direction of access unprotected, enabling unauthorized reads or writes to access-controlled peripherals.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full reglk_wrapper implementation details and the source/controllability of we_flag_0 in riscv_peripherals are not fully visible in the snippets, which could affect the exploitability assessment of the we_flag bypass. The exact bit semantics of reglk_ctrl for the read vs write asymmetry finding would benefit from the reglk_wrapper register map, but the asymmetry in bit indices is directly visible in SNIP-0009.

finding.summary: In hmac_wrapper, when debug_mode_i is asserted, the HMAC key (key0) and okey_hash are zeroed out rather than being protected, effectively bypassing the cryptographic key protection and allowing an attacker with debug mode access to force known-zero keys into HMAC operations.

evidence.description: Line 54: 'assign key = debug_mode_i ? 256'b0 : {key0[0]...key0[7]}' — when debug_mode_i is asserted, the HMAC key is forced to all-zeros instead of the stored key material. Line 56: 'assign okey_hash = debug_mode_i ? 256'b0 : {okey_hash_bytes[0]...}' — the outer key hash is also zeroed in debug mode. These assignments bypass the register lock (reglk_ctrl_i) protection for key material entirely when debug mode is active.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug mode unconditionally replaces cryptographic key material with zeros, bypassing reglk_ctrl protection and rendering HMAC operations cryptographically insecure under debug mode.

evidence.description: The hmac_wrapper instance receives reglk_ctrl[4*8+7:4*8] as reglk_ctrl_i and debug_mode_i as a separate input. The reglk_ctrl protection is present but the debug_mode_i bypass operates independently and overrides the key even when register lock bits are set.
evidence.supports_claim: The reglk_ctrl permission state is not consulted before the debug_mode_i override; the debug path nullifies key protection regardless of lock state.

evidence.description: ikey_hash_bytes assignment on line 55 does NOT include a debug_mode_i guard: 'assign ikey_hash = {ikey_hash_bytes[0]...ikey_hash_bytes[7]}' — while key and okey_hash are zeroed in debug mode, ikey_hash retains its stored value, creating an asymmetric and potentially exploitable inconsistency in the HMAC key schedule under debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: Inconsistent zeroing of HMAC key components in debug mode: key and okey_hash are zeroed but ikey_hash is not, creating an inconsistent security state.

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper exposes a jtag_unlock input and outputs reglk_ctrl_o from reglk_mem. The reglk_ctrl_o feeds the permission state for all peripherals including hmac_wrapper, but the debug_mode_i path in hmac_wrapper bypasses this lock mechanism entirely.
evidence.supports_claim: The register lock chain from reglk_wrapper is bypassed by debug_mode_i in hmac_wrapper without any interaction with the lock state.

finding.reasoning_summary: The hmac_wrapper module receives reglk_ctrl_i for register locking and debug_mode_i for debug control. Source evidence (SNIP-0009, lines 54 and 56) shows that when debug_mode_i is asserted, the HMAC key and outer key hash are unconditionally zeroed, independent of any register lock state. This means an attacker with debug mode access can force the HMAC to operate with all-zero keys regardless of what key material was programmed and regardless of whether register locks are set. Additionally, ikey_hash_bytes lacks the same debug_mode_i zeroing guard, creating an asymmetric key state. This represents a concrete bypass of the reglk_ctrl permission protection for cryptographic key material.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with debug mode access (debug_mode_i=1) can force the HMAC peripheral to use all-zero cryptographic keys, completely undermining message authentication. Any HMAC-protected operations become forgeable with knowledge of the zero key. The asymmetric treatment of ikey_hash vs key/okey_hash also creates an unpredictable cryptographic state that could be exploited for side-channel analysis or protocol attacks.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The mechanism by which debug_mode_i is asserted (hardware pin, privilege level, JTAG) is not shown in these snippets. The full hmac_wrapper read-side logic and key register write guards are not fully shown. However, the key zeroing assignment is directly visible in SNIP-0009 and constitutes unambiguous source evidence.

finding.summary: The acct_wrapper reset condition uses a logical OR of rst_ni and rst_6, meaning assertion of either signal independently triggers a full reset of the access-control memory (acct_mem) to all-ones (0xFFFFFFFF). Because rst_6 is a separate, software/peripheral-controllable reset signal distinct from the system reset rst_ni, an attacker or privileged software with access to the reset controller can force acct_mem to the all-ones state, which maximally permissive value is then directly forwarded as acc_ctrl_o to the system access-control logic.

evidence.description: The always block at line 79 resets acct_mem to 32'hffffffff for every entry when ~(rst_ni && ~rst_6) is true, i.e., whenever rst_6 is asserted OR rst_ni is de-asserted. This makes rst_6 alone sufficient to clear and repopulate all access-control entries with all-ones.
evidence.supports_claim: Asserting rst_6 resets all acct_mem entries to the all-ones value 0xFFFFFFFF without requiring the system reset rst_ni.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_o is directly driven from acct_mem: assign acc_ctrl_o = {acct_mem[3*0+2], acct_mem[3*0+1], acct_mem[3*0+0]|{8{we_flag}}}. After rst_6 forces acct_mem to all-ones, acc_ctrl_o carries the all-ones pattern to the system access-control bus.
evidence.supports_claim: The access-control output derives directly from acct_mem; resetting acct_mem via rst_6 sets acc_ctrl_o to all-ones, granting maximum privilege.

evidence.description: rst_6 from riscv_peripherals is connected to the acct_wrapper instance port rst_6 in the acct_wrapper instantiation block. This confirms rst_6 is a peripheral-level, software-reachable signal that flows into the security-critical reset path.
evidence.supports_claim: rst_6 is a peripheral-controlled signal fed directly into the acct_wrapper reset condition.

evidence.description: rst_6 is declared among a series of numbered reset controller signals (rst_1 through rst_13) in riscv_peripherals, indicating it is part of a programmable reset controller rather than a hardened boot-time reset. This makes it software-accessible.
evidence.supports_claim: rst_6 originates from a software-accessible reset controller, not an immutable hardware reset.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl is a 4*NB_PERIPHERALS-wide bus that aggregates access-control values from acct_wrapper and is used throughout riscv_peripherals to gate peripheral access. All-ones in acc_ctrl_o disables all restrictions.
evidence.supports_claim: The acc_ctrl bus fed from acct_wrapper's acc_ctrl_o governs peripheral access control; forcing it to all-ones bypasses all permission checks.

evidence.description: The reset condition ~(rst_ni && ~rst_6) is equivalent to (~rst_ni || rst_6), meaning rst_6 alone can trigger the destructive reset of acct_mem to 0xFFFFFFFF. There is no privilege check or lock guard gating whether rst_6 is allowed to reset the access-control memory. The reglk_ctrl bits only protect individual writes during normal operation but do not prevent the rst_6-triggered reset from overwriting the entire acct_mem.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing guard: rst_6 can be asserted by software to reset all access-control state to all-ones without any lock or privilege check preventing it.

evidence.description: After acct_mem is reset to all-ones via rst_6, acc_ctrl_o propagates the all-ones pattern. The acct_ctrl_i-gated enable (en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i) and the reglk_ctrl write-locks protect individual register writes but cannot prevent the rst_6 reset path from making all access-control entries maximally permissive, effectively granting all privilege levels full access to all peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: Exploiting rst_6 lets software elevate access to all peripherals by resetting the hardware permission table to all-ones.

finding.reasoning_summary: The acct_wrapper uses the compound reset expression ~(rst_ni && ~rst_6) which fires on either system reset de-assertion OR rst_6 assertion. When triggered, every entry in acct_mem is written to 0xFFFFFFFF, and this value is immediately reflected on acc_ctrl_o. Since rst_6 is a software-reachable peripheral reset signal (not the global chip reset), any code capable of asserting rst_6 can force all access-control memory to the maximally permissive all-ones state. The reglk_ctrl register-lock mechanism only blocks individual AXI writes during normal operation; it has no effect on the reset path. There is no privilege or lock guard around the rst_6-triggered reset, constituting a concrete permission-bypass vulnerability.
finding.security_impact: Software with the ability to assert rst_6 via the reset controller peripheral can reset all access-control table entries to 0xFFFFFFFF, propagating all-ones through acc_ctrl_o to the system access-control bus and granting unrestricted access to all protected peripherals, bypassing the entire access-control enforcement mechanism.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The mechanism by which software sets rst_6 (i.e., the reset controller register map) is not fully shown in the provided snippets; however, the declaration of rst_6 among a numbered series of software reset signals (rst_1 through rst_13) strongly implies software controllability. The exact privilege level required to set rst_6 is also not shown, but given it is a peripheral-level reset rather than a global hardware reset, it is likely accessible from machine or supervisor mode software.

finding.summary: On reset, acct_mem (access control table) is initialized to 0xFFFFFFFF (all-ones/all-access-granted), and reglk_mem (register lock table) is initialized to 0x0 (all-unlocked). Both conditions expose a window where all permissions are either fully open or fully unlocked immediately after reset, before software re-configures the registers.

evidence.description: On reset (rst_ni deasserted or rst_6 asserted), every entry in acct_mem is set to 32'hffffffff. This means all access control bits are set to 1 (permit everything) at reset, creating a post-reset window during which access is fully open before software writes the intended policy.
evidence.supports_claim: acct_mem reset value of all-ones means the access control table defaults to fully permissive state at every reset.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_o is derived directly from acct_mem values with assign acc_ctrl_o = {acct_mem[3*0+2], acct_mem[3*0+1], acct_mem[3*0+0]|{8{we_flag}}}. After reset, acct_mem is all-ones, so acc_ctrl_o immediately drives all-ones to downstream peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: The access control output fed to all peripherals is 0xFFFFFFFF at reset, meaning all peripheral access is granted.

evidence.description: On reset (rst_ni deasserted, jtag_unlock asserted, or rst_9 asserted), reglk_mem is set to 0x0. Since reglk_ctrl_o is derived from reglk_mem, all register lock bits are cleared to 0 at reset, meaning all registers in all peripherals are unlocked.
evidence.supports_claim: reglk_mem reset to zero means all peripheral register lock controls are deasserted at reset, allowing unrestricted writes to protected registers.

evidence.description: The jtag_unlock signal is included in the reset condition: ~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9). This means asserting jtag_unlock alone (without a system reset) clears all reglk_mem lock bits to zero, bypassing all register lock protections established by software.
evidence.supports_claim: A JTAG unlock signal can unilaterally clear all register lock state, bypassing software-configured access control locks without requiring a system reset.

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl_o = {reglk_mem[5],...,reglk_mem[0]}. When reglk_mem is zero (after reset or jtag_unlock), reglk_ctrl_o is all zeros, so no register lock bits are set, and all peripheral register write and read guards (e.g., reglk_ctrl[5], reglk_ctrl[1], etc. in acct_wrapper) will pass without restriction.
evidence.supports_claim: All downstream peripheral lock guards read from reglk_ctrl which is zero after reset/jtag_unlock, disabling all protection.

evidence.description: Write guards in acct_wrapper check reglk_ctrl[5], reglk_ctrl[13], reglk_ctrl[1], reglk_ctrl[7]; read guards check reglk_ctrl[4], reglk_ctrl[2], reglk_ctrl[0], reglk_ctrl[6]. All are zero at reset, so neither read nor write is blocked during the post-reset window.
evidence.supports_claim: All register lock guards in acct_wrapper are ineffective when reglk_ctrl is zero, enabling unrestricted read and write of acct_mem.

finding.reasoning_summary: Two concrete vulnerabilities exist. First, acct_mem (the access control table) resets to all-ones (0xFFFFFFFF), immediately granting all access permissions to all peripherals. Second, reglk_mem (the register lock table) resets to zero and can also be cleared by asserting jtag_unlock at any time without a full system reset. When reglk_mem is zero, all register write/read lock guards across the peripheral subsystem are simultaneously defeated. The jtag_unlock bypass is particularly severe because it allows a debug interface to wipe all software-established lock state without triggering a system reset, and after clearing reglk_mem, an attacker can rewrite acct_mem to grant arbitrary access control permissions.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with JTAG access can assert jtag_unlock to clear all register lock bits, then rewrite the access control table (acct_mem) to grant unauthorized access to any peripheral. Additionally, every system reset creates a window where all peripherals are fully accessible before software re-programs the access control and lock registers, which could be exploited via a reset injection attack.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full jtag_unlock signal source and its authentication/gating logic are not present in the provided snippets, so it is not possible to determine whether jtag_unlock itself is protected. However, the direct use of jtag_unlock in the reset condition of the security-critical reglk_mem register is confirmed by source evidence.

finding.summary: The HMAC wrapper zeroes out the cryptographic key when debug_mode_i is asserted, leaking the key state and weakening HMAC security during debug sessions. Additionally, the en signal in hmac_wrapper is derived from en_acct AND acct_ctrl_i, but the write-path register assignments (data[], startHash, newMessage) only check 'en && we' without also verifying the reglk_ctrl_i lock bits for write protection, unlike the read path which applies reglk guards.

evidence.description: Line 54: 'assign key = debug_mode_i ? 256'b0 : {...key0[]}' forces the HMAC key to all-zeros when debug_mode_i is asserted. Line 56 does the same for okey_hash. This means any entity that can assert debug_mode_i can zero out the HMAC key material, bypassing the cryptographic protection of the HMAC unit.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive key material is unconditionally overridden to zero when debug_mode_i is set, representing a debug-controlled bypass of the HMAC key protection.

evidence.description: The write path at line 123 ('else if(en && we)') gates writes only on en and we signals. The en signal is derived from en_acct && acct_ctrl_i (access control check), but there is no reglk_ctrl_i-based lock check on the write side for data[] registers (lines 106-121), unlike the read path in other modules that checks reglk bits. The key registers (key0, ikey_hash_bytes, okey_hash_bytes) write paths are also not shown with reglk guards in this snippet.
evidence.supports_claim: Write-side accesses to HMAC data registers lack the register-lock (reglk_ctrl_i) guard that would prevent modification after lock-down.

evidence.description: The hmac_wrapper instantiation at lines 1535-1536 passes reglk_ctrl_i from reglk_ctrl[4*8+7:4*8] and acct_ctrl_i from acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][4], confirming the access control path into the HMAC module.
evidence.supports_claim: The acct_ctrl_i signal is privilege-level indexed, meaning privilege level controls HMAC access, but debug_mode_i can still override key values.

evidence.description: Lines 37-39 define key0, ikey_hash_bytes, and okey_hash_bytes as the HMAC key material registers. These are the cryptographic secrets that should be protected from disclosure or modification.
evidence.supports_claim: The key material is a sensitive protected resource that is improperly exposed via debug_mode_i override.

evidence.description: debug_mode_i (line 22) is an input port that directly controls key material assignment (lines 54, 56), allowing the debug interface to bypass normal key protection without any additional authentication or privilege check visible in this module.

finding.reasoning_summary: The hmac_wrapper accepts a debug_mode_i signal that, when asserted, replaces the actual HMAC key (key0 registers) and okey_hash with 256'b0. This means any attacker who can assert debug_mode_i can force all HMAC operations to use a known all-zero key, completely defeating the cryptographic protection. The source snippet (SNIP-0011, lines 54 and 56) explicitly shows this bypass. Furthermore, the write-side register path (SNIP-0012) for data registers does not apply reglk_ctrl_i lock checks, unlike the acct_wrapper read path which does check reglk bits per register. The access control signal (acct_ctrl_i) correctly gates module enable via en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i, but the debug override is independent of this gating.
finding.security_impact: An adversary with the ability to assert debug_mode_i can cause all HMAC computations to use a zero key, making HMAC outputs predictable and forgeable. This breaks the authentication/integrity guarantees of any protocol relying on this HMAC unit. The missing write-side reglk guards also mean that after a supposed lock-down, the data registers can still be overwritten if access control permits entry.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full write-side handling for key0, ikey_hash_bytes, and okey_hash_bytes registers is not shown in the provided snippets (only data[] write resets are shown in SNIP-0012); it is possible those have additional guards not captured here. The mechanism by which debug_mode_i is asserted and who controls it is also not shown.

finding.summary: AXI protection bits (aw.prot and ar.prot) are permanently tied to zero when driving the HMAC wrapper, disabling AXI bus-level privilege/security/instruction-type protection signaling.

evidence.description: Lines 1591 and 1602 permanently assign hmac_axi_req.aw.prot and hmac_axi_req.ar.prot to 3'b000. The AXI PROT field encodes privileged access (bit 0), secure vs. non-secure access (bit 1), and instruction vs. data access (bit 2). Forcing all bits to zero marks every transaction as unprivileged, non-secure data access regardless of the actual requester context.
evidence.supports_claim: The HMAC peripheral, which handles cryptographic key material and digest output, receives AXI transactions with protection attributes permanently cleared. Any downstream logic or interconnect that would gate access based on PROT bits (e.g., a TrustZone-aware interconnect filtering non-secure accesses) will see all requests as unprivileged and non-secure, potentially allowing an unprivileged/non-secure agent to masquerade as privileged/secure.

evidence.description: The hmac_wrapper instance receives reglk_ctrl_i, acct_ctrl_i, and debug_mode_i as access-control inputs, showing the design intends to enforce privilege-level and lock-based access control. The AXI request bus (hmac_axi_req) feeds directly into axi_req_i of this wrapper.
evidence.supports_claim: The presence of explicit privilege and lock controls at the wrapper level confirms the HMAC is a security-sensitive peripheral, making the erasure of PROT bits on the AXI bus a meaningful weakening of layered protection.

evidence.description: hmac_wrapper.sv declares axi_req_i as the full AXI request input including all channel fields. The module also accepts reglk_ctrl_i (register lock) and acct_ctrl_i (account/access control), indicating it is designed as a protected cryptographic resource.
evidence.supports_claim: The HMAC core is an explicitly protected cryptographic resource; incorrect PROT values on its AXI interface undermine bus-fabric-level access enforcement.

finding.reasoning_summary: The source snippets show that all 20 tie-off assignments for hmac_axi_req hard-wire aw.prot and ar.prot to zero. AXI PROT[1] (Non-secure/Secure) is the standard signal used by TrustZone-aware interconnects to enforce security boundaries. Forcing it to zero marks all HMAC transactions as non-secure, which means a secure-world check on the interconnect would fail or be bypassed, and a non-secure-world check would always pass. Given that the HMAC wrapper already has register-lock and access-control inputs suggesting it is a security-sensitive component, having the bus-level protection attributes stripped is a concrete permission violation.
finding.security_impact: An attacker operating in a non-secure or unprivileged domain may be able to access the HMAC peripheral through an interconnect that would otherwise block non-secure transactions from reaching it, because the PROT bits are permanently set to indicate non-secure/unprivileged. Conversely, secure-world software using the HMAC will have its requests mislabeled as non-secure, potentially causing spurious access denials or bypassing secure-only guards elsewhere. In the worst case this exposes HMAC key material or allows unauthenticated hash computation.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Whether the system interconnect actually enforces PROT-based filtering is not visible from the provided snippets. If no TrustZone-aware firewall sits between the AXI master and the HMAC wrapper, the zero PROT value is benign from an enforcement standpoint. Additionally, the acct_ctrl_i and reglk_ctrl_i internal checks within hmac_wrapper may provide sufficient compensation, but their implementation is not included in the snippets.

The source snippets provide concrete evidence of missing resets and inconsistent debug guards on cryptographic key material.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can trigger a soft reset and then read HMAC registers may recover previously programmed key material from key0 registers. In debug mode, ikey_hash derived from the secret key is still computed and potentially readable, partially leaking key information even when key output is nominally zeroed.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The read-side logic of hmac_wrapper for key0 registers is not shown in the provided snippets (SNIP-0012 cuts off at line 149 before key0 write cases). It is unclear whether reglk_ctrl_i bits fully gate key0 reads. The full write handler for key0 addresses is also not visible, so it is uncertain whether key0 writes also lack lock protection.

finding.summary: The HMAC key is zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted, but the raw key registers (key0[]) remain intact and readable via the register read path, and the ikey_hash is never zeroed in debug mode, creating a sensitive data readback vulnerability.

evidence.description: The key signal is assigned from key0[] registers and conditionally zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted: 'assign key = debug_mode_i ? 256'b0 : {key0[0],...,key0[7]}'. However, key0[] registers themselves are not cleared, and ikey_hash is not zeroed in debug mode: 'assign ikey_hash = {ikey_hash_bytes[0],...}' has no debug_mode_i guard.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i zeroes the key output signal and okey_hash, but does not zero ikey_hash, leaving inner key hash data exposed in debug mode.

evidence.description: The read-side logic at line 226 guards the status register with reglk_ctrl_i[0], but the key register readback path (addresses 26-33 for key0[] and 34-41 for ikey_hash_bytes) is governed only by reglk_ctrl_i bits, not by debug_mode_i. When reglk_ctrl_i[4] (key read lock) is not set, the raw key0[] bytes and ikey_hash_bytes are directly readable via the register interface even in debug mode. The ikey_hash assignment at line 55 is completely unguarded by debug_mode_i.
evidence.supports_claim: The raw key0[] registers and ikey_hash_bytes are readable through the register interface without a debug_mode_i guard, while only the derived 'key' and 'okey_hash' output signals are suppressed in debug mode.

evidence.description: Line 54: 'assign key = debug_mode_i ? 256'b0 : {...key0[]}' and line 56: 'assign okey_hash = debug_mode_i ? 256'b0 : {okey_hash_bytes[]}'. Line 55: 'assign ikey_hash = {ikey_hash_bytes[0],...}' - ikey_hash has NO debug_mode_i guard. This inconsistency means debug mode protection is incomplete.
evidence.supports_claim: Asymmetric debug_mode_i protection: key and okey_hash are zeroed but ikey_hash is not, and none of the underlying storage registers are zeroed.

evidence.description: Key write protection uses reglk_ctrl_i[5] (lines 165-211) and key read protection uses reglk_ctrl_i[4] (not shown in read path snippet but implied). The read-side case at address 0 (line 226) uses reglk_ctrl_i[0], data reads use reglk_ctrl_i[2], but no debug_mode_i masking is applied to key register reads in the read-side always block.
evidence.supports_claim: The read-side register map does not apply debug_mode_i masking, so if reglk_ctrl is not set to lock the key registers, a debug-mode attacker can still read raw key material.

evidence.description: The hmac_wrapper is instantiated in riscv_peripherals with debug_mode_i passed through directly from the top-level input, and reglk_ctrl_i connected to reglk_ctrl[4*8+7:4*8]. The register lock and debug mode signals are separate controls, and if reglk_ctrl is not properly set, debug mode alone does not prevent key readback.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i and reglk_ctrl_i are independent; if reglk_ctrl does not lock the key read path, debug mode assertion does not fully protect key material from readback.

finding.reasoning_summary: In hmac_wrapper, the 'key' output and 'okey_hash' are zeroed when debug_mode_i is asserted (lines 54, 56 of SNIP-0004). However, (1) the underlying key0[] registers are not cleared on debug mode entry - they retain the key material; (2) ikey_hash (line 55) has no debug_mode_i guard at all; (3) the register read-side logic (SNIP-0005) uses only reglk_ctrl_i bits for gating reads, not debug_mode_i. This means that in debug mode, if register locks (reglk_ctrl) are not independently set, an attacker can directly read key0[] and ikey_hash_bytes[] through the register interface. Even if they are locked, ikey_hash leaks through to any consumer that uses it since it lacks the debug_mode_i zero assignment.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with debug mode access can read raw HMAC key material (key0 registers) and inner key hash (ikey_hash) through the register interface, bypassing the debug_mode_i protection that only zeros the combinational 'key' and 'okey_hash' output signals. This compromises the confidentiality of the HMAC secret key.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full read-side case statement for key register addresses (26-49) is not shown in the snippet, but the pattern from the visible portion (lines 226-238) shows no debug_mode_i masking. It is possible that key read addresses have additional guards not visible in the provided snippet range, but the ikey_hash assignment missing debug_mode_i is directly confirmed in source.

finding.summary: key_hash_bypass is not reset to a safe (0) value on system reset, and it is controlled by the same reglk_ctrl_i[1] bit that guards startHash and newMessage. When debug_mode_i is asserted, the HMAC key is forced to zero, effectively bypassing key protection. Additionally, key_hash_bypass itself is never cleared in the reset block, meaning a stale bypass value can persist across resets.

evidence.description: In the reset block (lines 102-121), startHash and newMessage are cleared to 0, but key_hash_bypass is NOT reset. This means after a soft reset via rst_4 or rst_ni, the key_hash_bypass flag retains its pre-reset value, potentially keeping the bypass active.
evidence.supports_claim: key_hash_bypass is not initialized to a safe value on reset, violating the principle that security-critical flags must be cleared on reset.

evidence.description: At line 130, key_hash_bypass is conditionally written: `key_hash_bypass <= reglk_ctrl_i[1] ? key_hash_bypass : wdata[2];`. This means when reglk_ctrl_i[1] is 0 (register not locked), any bus master can set key_hash_bypass by writing bit 2 of the control register at address offset 0.
evidence.supports_claim: The guard is only the register-lock bit; if the register lock is not engaged, key_hash_bypass can be set by an unprivileged write.

evidence.description: At line 54, the HMAC key is assigned: `assign key = debug_mode_i ? 256'b0 : {key0[0]...key0[7]};`. When debug_mode_i is asserted, the key is forced to all-zeros regardless of any lock state, effectively bypassing key confidentiality protection. Similarly at line 56, okey_hash is zeroed in debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: Asserting debug_mode_i forces the HMAC key material to zero, bypassing the key protection mechanism entirely and allowing an adversary with debug access to trivially predict HMAC outputs.

evidence.description: The hmac_wrapper instantiation at line 1536 passes `acct_ctrl_i (acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][4])` and debug_mode_i directly, confirming debug mode is a live input to hmac_wrapper that influences key selection.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i flows from outside the security perimeter into the key assignment logic with no additional guard.

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl is a combined lock bus produced by reglk_wrapper and fanned out to all peripherals. When reglk_ctrl[4*8+1] (bit 1 of the HMAC slice) is 0, the key_hash_bypass field is writable by any bus transaction that passes the en&&we check in hmac_wrapper.
evidence.supports_claim: The lock bit controlling key_hash_bypass is a runtime-configurable register, not a hardware-fixed constant, so it can be cleared to enable bypass writes.

finding.reasoning_summary: Two distinct permission-security violations exist in this chain. First, key_hash_bypass is never assigned in the reset branch of the always block (lines 102-121 of hmac_wrapper.sv), while startHash and newMessage are both explicitly reset. A reset (either rst_ni or rst_4) therefore does not clear the bypass flag, allowing a previously set bypass to survive a soft reset. Second, when debug_mode_i is high, the HMAC key (key and okey_hash) are unconditionally forced to 256'b0, meaning anyone with debug-mode access can predict or forge all HMAC outputs without knowing the actual key. Both issues undermine the confidentiality and integrity guarantees of the HMAC peripheral.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can assert debug_mode_i (e.g., via JTAG) receives a zeroed HMAC key, making all HMAC outputs trivially predictable and forgeable. An attacker who can write the HMAC control register before the lock is engaged (reglk_ctrl_i[1]==0) can set key_hash_bypass=1, and this flag persists across soft resets, permanently altering HMAC behavior until a full power-on reset.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact semantics of key_hash_bypass (how it is consumed downstream by the SHA256 core) are not shown in the provided snippets, so the precise impact of the bypass flag itself is inferred from its name and control path. The upstream enforcement of debug_mode_i (whether it requires authentication) is also outside the provided snippets.

finding.summary: In hmac_wrapper, the HMAC key registers (key0) and sensitive control signals (startHash, newMessage, key_hash_bypass) are reset to zero on rst_4, but the ikey_hash_bytes register writes are guarded by reglk_ctrl_i[5] yet the key itself is zeroed via debug_mode_i bypass. More critically, line 54 shows that when debug_mode_i is asserted, the key is unconditionally set to 256'b0, completely bypassing whatever key was programmed into key0[], allowing an attacker with debug access to force a known-key HMAC computation.

evidence.description: Line 54: `assign key = debug_mode_i ? 256'b0 : {key0[0],...,key0[7]};` — when debug_mode_i is asserted, the HMAC key is unconditionally replaced with all-zeros, bypassing the programmed key0 registers regardless of any register-lock state.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i completely overrides the HMAC key with a known constant (zero), allowing any entity that can assert debug mode to force HMAC computations with a predictable key.

evidence.description: Line 56: `assign okey_hash = debug_mode_i ? 256'b0 : {okey_hash_bytes[0],...};` — similarly, the outer key hash is zeroed in debug mode, further undermining HMAC integrity in debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: Both HMAC key material paths (key and okey_hash) are zeroed when debug_mode_i is active, reducing authentication to a fixed known-key operation.

evidence.description: Line 22: `input logic debug_mode_i;` is received as a plain input with no privilege check or lock gating before being used to override key material on lines 54 and 56.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i is an unguarded external input that directly controls whether real or zeroed key material is used in HMAC.

evidence.description: Lines 165-179: key0 registers are write-protected by reglk_ctrl_i[5], but this protection is irrelevant once debug_mode_i can zero the key at the assignment level.
evidence.supports_claim: Register-lock protection on key0 is bypassed by the debug_mode_i key-zeroing path, making the lock ineffective for protecting HMAC key confidentiality during debug mode.

evidence.description: Lines 1535-1536: hmac_wrapper is instantiated with reglk_ctrl_i and debug_mode_i passed as separate inputs, confirming debug_mode_i reaches the module from riscv_peripherals without additional gating.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug_mode_i signal flows from the top-level riscv_peripherals directly into hmac_wrapper where it overrides key material.

evidence.description: Lines 102-122: Reset condition `~(rst_ni && ~rst_4)` clears startHash, newMessage, and data[] to zero but does NOT clear key0, ikey_hash_bytes, or okey_hash_bytes, meaning key material persists across soft resets triggered by rst_4.
evidence.supports_claim: Key registers survive the rst_4-triggered soft reset, creating a key persistence issue where stale key material remains after a security-relevant reset event.

finding.reasoning_summary: The hmac_wrapper contains two distinct permission-related vulnerabilities supported by source evidence: (1) debug_mode_i unconditionally zeros the HMAC key (key and okey_hash assignments on lines 54/56 of SNIP-0010), bypassing all register-lock protections and forcing computations with a known zero key when debug mode is active; (2) the soft reset via rst_4 (lines 102-122 of SNIP-0011) clears data input registers but not the key registers (key0, ikey_hash_bytes, okey_hash_bytes), so key material persists across rst_4 resets. The reglk_ctrl_i lock bits protect key0 from writes but cannot prevent the debug_mode_i bypass at the combinational assignment level.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can assert debug_mode_i can force HMAC computations to use an all-zero key, defeating message authentication and potentially allowing forgery of HMAC tags. Additionally, key registers surviving rst_4 soft resets may leak sensitive key material across security domain transitions.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact privilege requirements to assert debug_mode_i at the system level are not shown in the provided snippets (the signal is passed in from riscv_peripherals but the source of debug_mode_i at the top level is not in scope). The full key0 and ikey_hash_bytes reset behavior would require confirming no other reset path clears them.

finding.summary: The HMAC wrapper's read-data output (rdata) and the key assignment logic expose sensitive key material when debug_mode_i is asserted: the key signal is forced to 256'b0 but ikey_hash is NOT zeroed in debug mode, allowing ikey_hash to leak. More critically, the okey_hash is zeroed in debug mode but ikey_hash is not guarded by debug_mode_i, creating an asymmetric protection gap. Additionally, the reglk_ctrl lock bits protect writes to key registers but the rdata output path lacks evidence of a read-lock guard checked against reglk_ctrl_i, enabling sensitive register readback regardless of the lock state.

evidence.description: Line 54: 'assign key = debug_mode_i ? 256'b0 : {...key0...}' zeros the key in debug mode. Line 55: 'assign ikey_hash = {ikey_hash_bytes[0],...}' has NO debug_mode_i guard — ikey_hash always reflects the stored inner key hash bytes regardless of debug mode. Line 56: okey_hash is zeroed in debug mode but ikey_hash is not, creating an asymmetric protection gap where ikey_hash leaks in debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: ikey_hash is not protected by debug_mode_i while key and okey_hash are, allowing inner key hash material to be read back in debug mode.

evidence.description: The write-side register lock (reglk_ctrl_i[1], [3], [5]) prevents writes to control, data, and key registers respectively. However, the rdata signal (the read output) is declared at line 52 but no snippet shows a corresponding read-lock check (e.g., reglk_ctrl_i[0], [2], [4]) applied before data is driven onto rdata. This means the lock mechanism protects writes but not reads of sensitive registers.
evidence.supports_claim: Write locks are implemented for HMAC registers but read-lock enforcement on rdata is absent from the provided source, allowing locked registers to be read back freely.

evidence.description: The hmac_wrapper is instantiated with reglk_ctrl_i connected to reglk_ctrl[4*8+7:4*8], which is the 8-bit lock control word for peripheral index 4. This confirms the lock bus is wired in, but the effectiveness depends on whether the read path inside hmac_wrapper actually enforces these bits.
evidence.supports_claim: Lock control is delivered to the HMAC wrapper but read-path enforcement is not evidenced in source snippets.

evidence.description: key0 (lines 37), ikey_hash_bytes (line 38), okey_hash_bytes (line 39), and hash (line 42) are sensitive cryptographic values declared as internal signals. The rdata output at line 52 is the path through which these values can be exfiltrated via register reads.
evidence.supports_claim: Cryptographic key material and hash outputs are the protected resources at risk of unauthorized readback.

evidence.description: debug_mode_i input at line 22 is used to zero the key (line 54) and okey_hash (line 56) but is NOT applied to ikey_hash (line 55), demonstrating incomplete debug-mode sanitization of sensitive outputs.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug mode control is inconsistently applied: ikey_hash is not sanitized, enabling key-derivative material leakage during debug.

finding.reasoning_summary: Two concrete permission-security violations exist in hmac_wrapper. First, the ikey_hash assignment (line 55) lacks the debug_mode_i guard present on key (line 54) and okey_hash (line 56), so inner key hash material is always exposed. Second, while write-side lock bits (reglk_ctrl_i[1,3,5]) are consistently checked before updating startHash, data[], key0[], and ikey_hash_bytes[], the rdata read-output path at line 52 has no corresponding read-lock enforcement visible in any source snippet, meaning any agent that can issue a read transaction can retrieve locked register values regardless of the reglk_ctrl state.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with AXI read access or in debug mode can read back cryptographic key material (key0, ikey_hash_bytes) that the register-lock mechanism is intended to protect. The ikey_hash leakage in debug mode could allow recovery of HMAC inner-key padding, compromising the HMAC security guarantee for any protected operation.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The rdata read-path multiplexer logic is not included in the provided source snippets (it would be in a section of hmac_wrapper.sv not shown). It is possible that read-lock guards exist in that section but are not visible here. The ikey_hash debug-mode gap is unambiguous from SNIP-0009 lines 54-56.

finding.summary: In hmac_wrapper, when debug_mode_i is asserted the HMAC key and okey_hash are forced to zero (256'b0), effectively nullifying the cryptographic secret material and allowing an attacker with debug-mode access to predict or bypass HMAC authentication.

evidence.description: Line 54: `assign key = debug_mode_i ? 256'b0 : {key0[0],...,key0[7]};` and line 56: `assign okey_hash = debug_mode_i ? 256'b0 : {okey_hash_bytes[...]}` — when debug_mode_i is high, the HMAC key and outer-key hash are replaced with all-zero constants.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug mode overrides the HMAC secret key with a known-zero value, destroying confidentiality of the key material.

evidence.description: The hmac_wrapper instantiation at line 1535-1537 connects reglk_ctrl[4*8+7:4*8] as reglk_ctrl_i and passes debug_mode_i directly from the top-level port into hmac_wrapper without any privilege check or reglk gating on the debug_mode_i path.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i is passed from the top level into hmac_wrapper with no register-lock or privilege-level guard preventing its activation.

evidence.description: The write-side logic (lines 123-128) uses reglk_ctrl_i[1] to protect startHash writes, showing that register-lock bits do guard write paths. However, the debug_mode_i zeroing of key/okey_hash (SNIP-0010) is a separate combinational override that completely bypasses those register locks.
evidence.supports_claim: Register-lock protects register writes but does not protect against the debug_mode_i key-zeroing path, leaving key material unprotected when debug mode is active.

evidence.description: ikey_hash (line 55) is assembled from ikey_hash_bytes without any debug_mode_i zeroing, creating an asymmetry: ikey_hash retains real key material in debug mode while key and okey_hash are zeroed. This inconsistency means the inner-key hash leaks in debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: Asymmetric debug_mode_i handling leaves ikey_hash readable with real content while key and okey_hash are zeroed, potentially enabling sensitive readback of intermediate key state.

evidence.description: With key forced to 256'b0 in debug mode, any HMAC computation produces a predictable, attacker-known result. An adversary who can assert debug_mode_i can forge valid HMAC tags for arbitrary messages.
evidence.supports_claim: Complete loss of HMAC integrity protection when debug mode is active.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0010 directly shows that when debug_mode_i is asserted, the HMAC key (key) and outer key hash (okey_hash) are unconditionally replaced with 256'b0. This is a debug backdoor that nullifies the cryptographic key. The reglk_ctrl_i register-lock mechanism protects register-write operations (SNIP-0011) but does not intercept this combinational key-zeroing path. Furthermore, ikey_hash is not subject to the same zeroing, creating an inconsistency that may leak intermediate key material. The debug_mode_i signal is wired directly from the top-level instantiation (SNIP-0004) without any privilege or lock gating.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can activate debug mode can (a) cause all HMAC operations to use a zero key, making all MACs forgeable with known inputs, and (b) potentially read back real ikey_hash bytes while key/okey_hash are zeroed, leaking partial key information. This undermines all integrity and authentication guarantees provided by the HMAC peripheral.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is not shown in the provided snippets how debug_mode_i is gated at the system level (e.g., whether it requires a JTAG unlock or privileged mode transition). If debug_mode_i is itself protected by a strong hardware root-of-trust, the practical exploitability is reduced, but the architectural vulnerability in hmac_wrapper remains.

finding.summary: In hmac_wrapper, when debug_mode_i is asserted, the HMAC key and okey_hash are zeroed out (set to 256'b0), effectively bypassing the cryptographic key protection and allowing an attacker in debug mode to observe or use a known-zero key for HMAC operations.

evidence.description: Line 54: 'assign key = debug_mode_i ? 256'b0 : {key0[0]...key0[7]}' and line 56: 'assign okey_hash = debug_mode_i ? 256'b0 : {okey_hash_bytes[...]}' show that when debug_mode_i is active, the secret key and outer key hash are replaced with all-zeros, completely undermining HMAC security.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug mode forces HMAC key material to zero, bypassing key protection.

evidence.description: The HMAC key registers (key0[0..7]), ikey_hash_bytes, and okey_hash_bytes are intended to hold secret cryptographic key material used for HMAC computation.
evidence.supports_claim: Key material in hmac_wrapper is security-sensitive and should not be exposed or zeroed by debug control.

evidence.description: The register lock (reglk_ctrl_i) guards write access to HMAC registers (e.g., reglk_ctrl_i[1] guards startHash/newMessage/key_hash_bypass, reglk_ctrl_i[3] guards data registers). However, no reglk guard is applied to the key zeroing behavior triggered by debug_mode_i.
evidence.supports_claim: The reglk protection mechanism does not prevent debug_mode_i from overriding the key to zero.

evidence.description: The hmac_wrapper instance receives debug_mode_i directly from the top-level riscv_peripherals instantiation without any privilege-level gating or lock check before it is passed in.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i is connected without an intermediate guard to hmac_wrapper, enabling the key zeroing path.

evidence.description: ikey_hash (line 55) does NOT zero out in debug mode: 'assign ikey_hash = {ikey_hash_bytes[0]...}' has no debug_mode_i conditional, while key and okey_hash do. This asymmetry means that in debug mode, ikey_hash still holds real key material while key and okey_hash are zeroed, producing an inconsistent and insecure cryptographic state that may leak inner key hash bytes through observable HMAC outputs.
evidence.supports_claim: Inconsistent debug-mode zeroing leaves ikey_hash exposed while zeroing other key components, creating a security-inconsistent HMAC state.

finding.reasoning_summary: The source snippet SNIP-0010 shows that debug_mode_i unconditionally replaces the HMAC 'key' and 'okey_hash' signals with 256'b0, while 'ikey_hash' is computed without any debug_mode_i guard. This has two security implications: (1) an adversary who can assert debug_mode_i can force the HMAC to use a known-zero key, defeating authentication; (2) the asymmetric treatment of ikey_hash (no zeroing) vs. key and okey_hash (zeroed) creates an inconsistent cryptographic state that may leak real ikey_hash values through HMAC results computed with the zeroed outer components. No reglk or privilege check prevents this behavior in the snippets provided.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can assert debug_mode_i can: (1) force HMAC to use a zero key, breaking message authentication; (2) potentially extract real ikey_hash material through observable HMAC outputs since ikey_hash is not zeroed. This represents a complete bypass of HMAC key protection in debug mode.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is not shown how debug_mode_i itself is protected or who can assert it at the system level. If debug_mode_i is properly controlled by a secure debug authentication mechanism, the severity may be reduced, but the asymmetric treatment of ikey_hash vs. key/okey_hash remains a design flaw regardless.

finding.summary: On reset, the reglk_wrapper initializes all register-lock control memory (reglk_mem) to 0x0, which means all protection bits are cleared (unlocked) after reset. Downstream peripherals (hmac_wrapper, acct_wrapper, pkt_wrapper) rely on these lock values to guard writes to sensitive registers. A reset event therefore temporarily removes all register-lock protections across all peripherals.

evidence.description: On reset (~rst_ni || jtag_unlock || rst_9), reglk_mem[j] is set to 'h0 for all j. This clears all register-lock bits to zero, meaning every bit of reglk_ctrl_o becomes 0 (unlocked) immediately after reset or JTAG unlock.
evidence.supports_claim: Reset drives all lock-control registers to the unlocked state (0), removing all register protections.

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl_o is assembled directly from reglk_mem words: assign reglk_ctrl_o = {reglk_mem[5],...,reglk_mem[0]}. When reglk_mem is zeroed on reset, reglk_ctrl_o is entirely 0, propagating the unlocked state to all consumers.
evidence.supports_claim: The register-lock output signal is directly composed from the zeroed memory, so all downstream protection bits are 0 after reset.

evidence.description: riscv_peripherals connects reglk_wrapper.reglk_ctrl_o directly to the riscv_peripherals.reglk_ctrl signal, which is then distributed to hmac_wrapper, pkt_wrapper, and acct_wrapper as their reglk_ctrl_i.
evidence.supports_claim: The zeroed lock state propagates to all protected peripherals through reglk_ctrl.

evidence.description: acct_wrapper guards writes using reglk_ctrl bits (e.g., reglk_ctrl[5], reglk_ctrl[13], reglk_ctrl[1], reglk_ctrl[7]). When reglk_ctrl is 0 (post-reset), all guards evaluate to false, allowing writes to all acct_mem entries.
evidence.supports_claim: All acct_mem write guards are disabled when reglk_ctrl is 0.

evidence.description: hmac_wrapper holds sensitive cryptographic state including key0, ikey_hash_bytes, okey_hash_bytes, and hash output registers, all of which are writable through the AXI interface conditioned on reglk_ctrl_i bits.
evidence.supports_claim: Sensitive cryptographic registers are unprotected when reglk_ctrl_i is 0.

evidence.description: The reset condition also includes jtag_unlock as a trigger: ~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9). This means asserting jtag_unlock also clears all lock registers to 0, creating a JTAG-triggered unlock of all peripheral protections.
evidence.supports_claim: JTAG unlock signal clears all register-lock protections, not just JTAG-specific ones, broadening the attack surface.

finding.reasoning_summary: The reglk_wrapper is the root of the permission chain: it produces reglk_ctrl_o which governs write access to sensitive registers in hmac_wrapper, acct_wrapper, and pkt_wrapper. On reset (or jtag_unlock assertion), reglk_mem is initialized to 0, which sets all lock bits to 0 (unlocked). Since the downstream guard logic treats a 0 lock bit as 'not locked', all writes to protected registers are permitted immediately after reset. The secure initial state should be locked (all 1s or a non-zero protective value), not unlocked (0). This creates a window where an attacker who can trigger reset or jtag_unlock can modify otherwise protected register state.
finding.security_impact: An attacker able to trigger a reset or JTAG unlock can modify cryptographic keys, HMAC configuration, access-control tables, and packet key registers before the lock bits are re-programmed by firmware, potentially extracting secrets or bypassing access control enforcement.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full reglk_wrapper write logic beyond line 92 is not shown; it is possible additional lock-on registers exist. Additionally, it is unclear how quickly firmware re-programs the lock registers after reset, which affects the exploitability window. The hmac_wrapper key-write guard logic is referenced but its full body is not in the provided snippets.

finding.summary: In hmac_wrapper, when debug_mode_i is asserted the HMAC key (key0) and okey_hash are zeroed out, effectively bypassing key confidentiality and allowing the HMAC to operate with a known-zero key. Additionally, wdata is written to HMAC internal registers (data[], startHash, newMessage, key_hash_bypass) without any check on debug_mode_i or privilege level in the write path, meaning a debug-mode attacker can freely inject data into the HMAC engine.

evidence.description: Line 54: 'assign key = debug_mode_i ? 256'b0 : {key0[0],...}' and line 56: 'assign okey_hash = debug_mode_i ? 256'b0 : {okey_hash_bytes[0],...}' show that asserting debug_mode_i forces both the HMAC key and outer-key-hash to all-zeros, completely nullifying the secret key material.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i bypasses the HMAC key, reducing it to a known zero value and making HMAC output predictable/forgeable.

evidence.description: ikey_hash assignment on line 55 does NOT include a debug_mode_i guard: 'assign ikey_hash = {ikey_hash_bytes[0],...}' — the inner key hash is always passed through regardless of debug mode, creating an asymmetric protection gap compared to key and okey_hash.

Together, this allows an attacker with debug access to operate the HMAC with a known-zero key on attacker-chosen data, enabling HMAC forgery.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can assert debug_mode_i can force the HMAC key to all-zeros and freely write message data, enabling forgery of HMAC values. This undermines any authentication or integrity protection that relies on the HMAC peripheral's output.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact mechanism by which debug_mode_i is asserted in the system (e.g., JTAG, CSR) is not shown in these snippets, but the RTL effect is clear from the source code. finding.summary: The acct_wrapper's acc_ctrl_o output unconditionally ORs a we_flag into the lowest access-control word, allowing any write operation to permanently escalate permissions for peripheral 0 regardless of register-lock or access-control state.

evidence.description: Line 49: `assign acc_ctrl_o = {acct_mem[3*0+2], acct_mem[3*0+1], acct_mem[3*0+0]|{8{we_flag}}};` — the least-significant byte of the access-control output for peripheral index 0 is unconditionally OR-ed with 8 replications of we_flag. When we_flag is asserted (indicating a write is in progress), all 8 permission bits for that peripheral are forced to 1, bypassing any restriction stored in acct_mem[0] and any register-lock guard.
evidence.supports_claim: The OR with we_flag means that during any write transaction the full access-control byte is set to 0xFF for the affected peripheral, overriding both the reglk_ctrl write-protection check and the acct_ctrl_i enable gate.

evidence.description: Lines 87-111: write updates to acct_mem are correctly gated by `en && we` and per-entry reglk_ctrl bits. However, the combinational output (SNIP-0009 line 49) bypasses these guards by directly OR-ing we_flag into the output before the registered value takes effect.
evidence.supports_claim: The register-write guards on acct_mem do not protect the combinational output path, so the permission escalation via we_flag occurs even if the write to acct_mem itself is blocked by a lock bit.

evidence.description: Lines 215-222: acc_ctrl_c is derived directly from acc_ctrl (which is driven by acc_ctrl_o of acct_wrapper) and is then distributed to all peripheral wrappers (hmac, pkt, acct, reglk). Any elevation of acc_ctrl_o immediately propagates to all consumers.
evidence.supports_claim: Because acc_ctrl_c fans out to every peripheral's acct_ctrl_i, a transient we_flag-driven escalation in acct_wrapper affects the access-control enable for the entire peripheral set.

evidence.description: Lines 1626-1627 (pkt_wrapper instantiation) and line 1536 (hmac_wrapper instantiation) pass acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][n] as acct_ctrl_i. The inflated acc_ctrl value produced during a write therefore directly controls whether unprivileged code can access PKT fuse data and HMAC key material.
evidence.supports_claim: Peripheral access gating relies entirely on acc_ctrl_c, so the we_flag escalation translates directly into a transient permission grant for sensitive peripherals.

evidence.description: HMAC wrapper receives acct_ctrl_i derived from the escalated acc_ctrl_c. HMAC holds cryptographic key material (key0, ikey_hash, okey_hash registers in hmac_wrapper).
evidence.supports_claim: Transient we_flag escalation can open access to HMAC key registers for a software principal that should be denied.

finding.reasoning_summary: In acct_wrapper the combinational output acc_ctrl_o is computed as `acct_mem[0] | {8{we_flag}}` for the first peripheral slot (line 49 of acct_wrapper.sv). This means that whenever we_flag is high (a write transaction is active on the AXI-Lite bus), all 8 access-control bits for peripheral 0 are forced to 1 in the output, independent of (a) what is stored in acct_mem, (b) the register-lock bits in reglk_ctrl, and (c) the acct_ctrl_i enable signal. Because acc_ctrl_o feeds into riscv_peripherals.acc_ctrl, which is rearranged into acc_ctrl_c and then distributed to every peripheral wrapper as their acct_ctrl_i, this transient escalation propagates system-wide. A malicious or compromised user-space process performing any AXI write could exploit the window during which we_flag is high to gain access to otherwise-restricted peripherals such as the PKT fuse reader or HMAC key storage.
finding.security_impact: Transient but exploitable privilege escalation: during any write transaction to the acct_wrapper the access-control bits for at least peripheral 0 are elevated to fully-permitted (0xFF), potentially allowing an unprivileged principal to read or write sensitive peripheral registers (cryptographic keys in HMAC, fuse data in PKT, or the register-lock configuration in REGLK) within the same clock cycle window.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The exact definition and driver of we_flag are not shown in the provided snippets; it appears to be an input to acct_wrapper (line 31 of SNIP-0009) fed from an external source. If we_flag is always 0 in practice the exploit window would never open, but based on its name and usage it is intended to track write activity. The precise mapping of peripheral index 0 to a specific peripheral ID also requires cross-referencing the NB_PERIPHERALS parameterisation, which is partially visible but not fully resolved here.

finding.summary: AXI protection field (prot) is hardwired to zero for all PKT wrapper AXI requests, eliminating privilege/security-level signaling on the AXI bus

evidence.description: Both the write-channel protection field (pkt_axi_req.aw.prot) and the read-channel protection field (pkt_axi_req.ar.prot) are permanently tied to zero via continuous assignments. AXI PROT encodes privilege level, security state (Secure/Non-Secure), and instruction/data access type. Setting them to zero unconditionally means every transaction from the PKT wrapper appears as an unprivileged, secure, data access regardless of actual software privilege level.
evidence.supports_claim: The aw.prot and ar.prot fields are forced to '0 with no conditional logic, stripping all AXI privilege and security-level information from PKT wrapper bus transactions.

evidence.description: The pkt_axi_req signal is the AXI master request bundle driven into the pkt_wrapper peripheral, which handles fuse memory and key material (jtag_hash, okey_hash, ikey_hash). Access control to this peripheral depends on AXI protection attributes being correctly propagated.
evidence.supports_claim: PKT wrapper controls sensitive fuse/key memory; correct AXI protection signaling is required for downstream access-control enforcement.

evidence.description: pkt_wrapper accepts axi_req_i as its AXI request input alongside reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i. If downstream logic within pkt_wrapper or any AXI interconnect fabric uses the prot field to gate access, the hardwired zero value bypasses those checks.
evidence.supports_claim: The AXI request enters pkt_wrapper without privilege metadata, making any protection gating based on prot ineffective.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0002 shows that both aw.prot and ar.prot are unconditionally assigned '0 for all AXI transactions directed at the PKT wrapper. The AXI PROT[1:0] bits encode privilege level and Secure/Non-Secure state; setting them to zero makes every transaction appear as unprivileged and secure. Any AXI interconnect or slave that gates access based on the prot field will either always grant or always deny access regardless of actual requester privilege, defeating AXI-based privilege separation. The PKT wrapper handles sensitive fuse and key data (SNIP-0001, SNIP-0003), amplifying the impact of this missing protection.
finding.security_impact: All AXI transactions to the PKT fuse/key peripheral are sent with privilege and security-level bits cleared to zero. If any downstream fabric or the peripheral itself enforces access control via prot, those checks are rendered permanently ineffective. An unprivileged software agent whose transactions are indistinguishable (at the AXI level) from privileged ones could potentially read or write sensitive fuse key material.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source snippets do not show the interior of pkt_wrapper or the AXI interconnect to confirm whether prot-based filtering is actually implemented downstream. If no downstream component uses prot for access control the practical impact is limited to protocol non-compliance. The reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i guard paths inside pkt_wrapper are not shown.

finding.summary: The pkt_wrapper module exposes fuse_addr_o unconditionally without checking reglk_ctrl_i lock bits, allowing read access to fuse memory addresses even when register locks are set.

evidence.supports_claim: The fuse address output is a sensitive resource that should be gated by the register lock control.

evidence.description: In hmac_wrapper, write-side logic checks reglk_ctrl_i bits before updating internal registers (e.g., reglk_ctrl_i[1], reglk_ctrl_i[3], reglk_ctrl_i[5]). This establishes the pattern that reglk_ctrl_i should gate sensitive register updates.
evidence.supports_claim: The established security pattern uses reglk_ctrl_i to gate writes to sensitive registers, but this pattern is absent for the fuse_addr_o output in pkt_wrapper.

evidence.description: In acct_wrapper, writes to acct_mem are conditionally blocked using reglk_ctrl bits (e.g., reglk_ctrl[5], reglk_ctrl[13], reglk_ctrl[1], reglk_ctrl[7]). This is the reference guard pattern for the security architecture.
evidence.supports_claim: The security architecture relies on reglk_ctrl bit checks to protect sensitive register updates, a check that is absent for fuse address output in pkt_wrapper.

evidence.description: pkt_wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i [7:0] as an input but no source snippet shows fuse_addr_o being gated or zeroed based on any reglk_ctrl_i bit. evidence.supports_claim: fuse_addr_o is driven unconditionally without checking reglk_ctrl_i, representing a missing guard on a sensitive fuse memory access path.

finding.reasoning_summary: The design pattern in this RTL security architecture uses reglk_ctrl_i bits to gate writes (and sometimes reads) to sensitive registers in modules like hmac_wrapper and acct_wrapper. However, in pkt_wrapper, the fuse_addr_o output — which drives the address into fuse memory (a sensitive resource storing cryptographic key location data given the pkey_loc signal) — is not shown to be gated by any reglk_ctrl_i bit check. This creates an asymmetric protection: write locks are applied per the established pattern in other modules, but the fuse address output from pkt_wrapper lacks the corresponding lock-bit check.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or unprivileged software could trigger fuse memory reads through the pkt_wrapper interface even when register locks (reglk_ctrl) are intended to restrict access, potentially leaking the location or contents of cryptographic keys (pkey_loc) stored in fuse memory.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source snippets do not include the full pkt_wrapper body showing the read-side or fuse_addr_o drive logic beyond the port declarations. It is possible that guard logic exists in the pkt_wrapper implementation not captured in the provided snippets. finding.summary: fuse_rdata_i (fuse memory read data) flows into pkt_wrapper without any reglk_ctrl or acct_ctrl gating on the read path, enabling sensitive fuse data to be read back regardless of register-lock state.

'b0 : {31'b0, ready}' and 'data[0] <= reglk_ctrl_i[3] ? data[0] : wdata'). This demonstrates the intended pattern: reads return 0 when locked, writes are suppressed.

evidence.description: pkt_wrapper.sv lines 30-32 and 41-45 show that fuse_rdata_i is received as an input and rdata is an internal signal, but there is no visible conditional assignment of rdata that checks reglk_ctrl_i before exposing fuse_rdata_i on the AXI read response.
