evidence.supports_claim: Confirms that reglk_ctrl pass-through without enforcement is a recurring weakness in these wrappers.

finding.reasoning_summary: The design uses reglk_ctrl_i bits to gate both writes and reads in hmac_wrapper (SNIP-0010 shows explicit ternary guards on every register read and write). pkt_wrapper receives the same reglk_ctrl_i[7:0] and acct_ctrl_i signals (SNIP-0011, SNIP-0004), but the available source for pkt_wrapper does not show any corresponding guard expression protecting the fuse_rdata_i readback path. Because fuse memory holds secret provisioning data (private keys, device identity, etc.) and the lock mechanism is present but not applied to the fuse read path in pkt_wrapper, an unprivileged or locked-out software agent can still read fuse data through the AXI interface.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with AXI bus access can read raw fuse data (e.g., private keys stored at pkey_loc) even when reglk_ctrl locks are asserted, defeating the register-lock protection mechanism and exposing device secrets.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full pkt_wrapper read-side always block is not present in the provided snippets (only the port declarations and signal declarations are shown). It is possible that a guard exists in unlisted lines. However, the pattern visible in hmac_wrapper (explicit per-bit ternary guards) is entirely absent from the pkt_wrapper snippet, and the fuse_rdata_i signal is declared without any visible conditional logic referencing reglk_ctrl_i.

finding.summary: The pkt_wrapper module drives fuse_req_o to access fuse memory without verifying reglk_ctrl_i or acct_ctrl_i permission bits before issuing the fuse read request. While the module receives reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i signals, the fuse_req_o output path is not shown to be gated by any permission check, allowing fuse memory reads regardless of lock or access-control state.

evidence.supports_claim: Other wrappers gate sensitive register writes on reglk_ctrl; pkt_wrapper fuse_req_o has no equivalent guard shown.

evidence.description: In hmac_wrapper, register writes are gated by reglk_ctrl_i bits (e.g., reglk_ctrl_i[1], reglk_ctrl_i[3], reglk_ctrl_i[5]) before allowing updates to sensitive HMAC key and data registers. This confirms the pattern that permission checks must gate sensitive operations.
evidence.supports_claim: The design uses reglk_ctrl to protect HMAC keys and data; pkt_wrapper fuse reads lack equivalent protection gating.

evidence.description: pkt_wrapper receives reglk_ctrl_i (line 26) and acct_ctrl_i (line 27) and outputs fuse_req_o (line 30) and fuse_addr_o (line 31). No source snippet demonstrates that fuse_req_o is conditioned on reglk_ctrl_i or acct_ctrl_i being in a permissive state, constituting a missing guard on fuse memory request issuance.
evidence.supports_claim: fuse_req_o can be asserted without verifying lock or access-control permission, enabling unauthorized fuse reads.

finding.reasoning_summary: The pkt_wrapper module issues fuse memory read requests via fuse_req_o and fuse_addr_o. While it accepts reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i as permission inputs (consistent with the system-wide permission architecture seen in hmac_wrapper and acct_wrapper), no source snippet shows these signals being used to gate fuse_req_o. In contrast, both hmac_wrapper and acct_wrapper demonstrate explicit reglk_ctrl gating of sensitive register writes. The fuse_req_o path in pkt_wrapper is connected directly to the fuse memory bus at the riscv_peripherals level without any interposing check. This is a missing guard vulnerability on a security-critical resource (fuse/OTP memory containing public key location and potentially other provisioned secrets).
finding.security_impact: An attacker or unprivileged software able to trigger pkt_wrapper fuse requests could read fuse memory contents (e.g., provisioned public key material or security configuration) regardless of whether the register lock or access control bits have been set to deny access, bypassing the intended permission enforcement.

finding.summary: The HMAC wrapper zeroes out the HMAC key and okey_hash when debug_mode_i is asserted, effectively bypassing key confidentiality in debug mode. Additionally, the register-lock control (reglk_ctrl_i) is passed into hmac_wrapper but the write-side logic in the always block does not check reglk_ctrl_i before updating sensitive registers (startHash, newMessage, data), and the key registers (key0, ikey_hash_bytes, okey_hash_bytes) are not shown to be protected by reglk_ctrl in the write path.

evidence.description: Line 54: 'assign key = debug_mode_i ? 256'b0 : {key0[0],...}' forces the HMAC key to all-zeros when debug_mode_i is asserted. Line 56 does the same for okey_hash. This means any debug-mode access bypasses key confidentiality entirely.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_i bypasses the HMAC key, zeroing it unconditionally and exposing the HMAC computation to an attacker who can assert debug mode.

evidence.description: The write-side always block (lines 100-126) guards writes only on reset conditions and 'en && we'. There is no check of reglk_ctrl_i anywhere in this block before writing startHash, newMessage, or data registers. The reglk_ctrl_i input is declared (line 20 of SNIP-0009) but never read in the write path shown.
evidence.supports_claim: The register-lock signal reglk_ctrl_i is not consulted in the HMAC write path, so lock enforcement is absent for HMAC data registers.

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl[4*8+7:4*8] is passed to hmac_wrapper as reglk_ctrl_i. The reglk_ctrl bus originates from reglk_wrapper and is supposed to gate access to peripheral registers.
evidence.supports_claim: The reglk_ctrl slice for HMAC is wired in but not enforced within hmac_wrapper's write logic, making the lock ineffective.

evidence.description: reglk_ctrl_o is assembled from reglk_mem and distributed to all peripheral wrappers including hmac_wrapper. The lock is intended to prevent writes/reads to sensitive registers.
evidence.supports_claim: The lock mechanism exists architecturally but is not enforced in the HMAC write path.

evidence.description: ikey_hash is assigned without any debug_mode_i guard (line 55: 'assign ikey_hash = {ikey_hash_bytes[0],...}'), so ikey_hash is always readable from its backing registers regardless of debug mode, while key and okey_hash are zeroed. This asymmetry means ikey_hash leaks its real value in debug mode.
evidence.supports_claim: ikey_hash is not zeroed in debug mode unlike key and okey_hash, creating an inconsistent and potentially exploitable information leak.

finding.reasoning_summary: Two concrete vulnerabilities are present in hmac_wrapper. First, debug_mode_i unconditionally zeros the HMAC key and okey_hash (lines 54, 56 of SNIP-0009), allowing an attacker with debug access to bypass key protection. Second, ikey_hash lacks the same debug_mode_i guard (line 55), creating an asymmetric leak. Third, the write-side always block (SNIP-0010) does not check reglk_ctrl_i at any point, meaning the register-lock mechanism that is wired in from riscv_peripherals (SNIP-0003) provides no actual write protection for HMAC data registers. The acct_wrapper correctly checks reglk_ctrl bits before writes and reads (SNIP-0008), confirming that the pattern exists and was intentionally omitted or forgotten in hmac_wrapper.
finding.security_impact: An attacker who can assert debug_mode_i obtains a zero HMAC key, breaking all HMAC-based authentication. An attacker who can write to the HMAC peripheral (even after a lock should have been set) can overwrite data, startHash, and newMessage registers because reglk_ctrl_i is not enforced in the write path. Additionally, ikey_hash leaks in debug mode.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The full read-side logic of hmac_wrapper is not shown in the snippets, so it is unclear whether reglk_ctrl_i is used there. The write path for key0, ikey_hash_bytes, and okey_hash_bytes is also not shown, leaving open whether those have separate lock checks.

finding.summary: The reglk_wrapper reset condition includes jtag_unlock as a reset trigger, allowing JTAG to clear all register-lock values (reglk_mem) to zero, effectively disabling all peripheral access controls and register locks system-wide.

evidence.description: The reset condition in reglk_wrapper is `~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9)`, meaning asserting jtag_unlock forces all reglk_mem entries to 0x0. This clears all register lock bits, removing write and read protections for all peripherals.
evidence.supports_claim: JTAG unlock signal can be used to clear all register-lock state (reglk_mem), bypassing all peripheral protection locks.

evidence.description: reglk_mem holds the register lock values output as reglk_ctrl_o, which are distributed to all peripherals (hmac, acct, pkt, reglk) to control read/write access. Resetting reglk_mem to 0 removes all lock protections.
evidence.supports_claim: reglk_mem is the central permission state whose contents gate access to all secured peripheral registers.

evidence.description: reglk_wrapper is instantiated with jtag_unlock connected from riscv_peripherals, and reglk_ctrl_o feeds all peripheral wrappers as their register lock inputs.
evidence.supports_claim: The jtag_unlock path reaches reglk_wrapper directly and can trigger the security-clearing reset.

evidence.description: acct_wrapper uses reglk_ctrl bits to guard reads and writes to acct_mem entries. If reglk_ctrl is cleared to 0 by the JTAG-triggered reset, all these guards evaluate to false, allowing unrestricted read/write of access control memory.
evidence.supports_claim: Clearing reglk_mem removes write and read locks from all acct_mem entries in acct_wrapper.

evidence.description: hmac_wrapper uses reglk_ctrl_i bits to gate writes to hash data registers and key material. A zero reglk_ctrl (from JTAG-triggered reset) removes all these write locks.
evidence.supports_claim: Clearing reglk_mem disables all register-lock guards on HMAC key and data registers.

evidence.description: acc_ctrl_c is derived from acc_ctrl and gated by privilege level (priv_lvl_i) before being passed to peripherals as acct_ctrl_i. However, the register-lock layer (reglk_ctrl) is independently managed by reglk_wrapper and is defeated by the jtag_unlock reset path.
evidence.supports_claim: There is a separate privilege-based access control layer, but the register-lock layer protecting sensitive register writes is fully defeatable via jtag_unlock.

finding.reasoning_summary: The reglk_wrapper module holds register-lock state (reglk_mem) that controls read/write access to security-sensitive registers across all peripherals (HMAC key registers, access-control memory, PKT fuse data). Its reset condition (`~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9)`) treats jtag_unlock assertion identically to a system reset, zeroing all lock bits. With all reglk_mem entries at zero, all peripheral register guards that check `reglk_ctrl[N]` before allowing writes or reads will allow unrestricted access. An attacker with JTAG access can assert jtag_unlock to clear all locks and then freely modify or read all protected peripheral registers including HMAC keys and access-control tables.
finding.security_impact: An attacker with JTAG access can assert jtag_unlock to zero all register-lock values, bypassing all register-level access controls on HMAC key material, access-control tables, and PKT fuse data.
