finding.summary: Boot ROM data selection mux is broken: both branches of the ternary select the same `rom_rdata_linux` signal, making `ariane_boot_sel_i` have no effect and preventing bare-metal boot mode from ever being selected.

evidence.description: Line 534: `assign rom_rdata = (ariane_boot_sel_i) ? rom_rdata_linux : rom_rdata_linux;` — both the true and false branches assign `rom_rdata_linux`. The comment states this is supposed to switch to bare-metal mode when `ariane_boot_sel_i` is deasserted, but the bare-metal ROM data (`rom_rdata_bm`) is never used. The selection signal `ariane_boot_sel_i` is completely ignored.
evidence.supports_claim: The boot-mode selection control is non-functional: regardless of the value of the boot select input, the Linux boot ROM image is always served. An attacker or configuration error cannot be prevented from always booting the Linux image even when bare-metal mode is intended, and conversely there is no way to enforce bare-metal-only boot via this signal.

evidence.description: Three ROM read-data signals are declared: `rom_rdata`, `rom_rdata_bm`, and `rom_rdata_linux`. The existence of `rom_rdata_bm` confirms a separate bare-metal boot ROM image was intended to be selectable, but it is never driven to `rom_rdata`.
evidence.supports_claim: The bare-metal boot ROM data path (`rom_rdata_bm`) is dead code; the mux that should protect the boot-mode selection is completely ineffective.

finding.reasoning_summary: The continuous assignment on line 534 uses a ternary operator conditioned on `ariane_boot_sel_i` but assigns `rom_rdata_linux` on both branches. The declared signal `rom_rdata_bm` (visible in the declaration on line 490) is never connected to the output mux. This means the boot ROM selection control is permanently bypassed: the system always boots from the Linux image regardless of the `ariane_boot_sel_i` input. This is a security-relevant misconfiguration because boot-mode selection is a critical lifecycle/privilege boundary.
finding.security_impact: The boot security boundary is broken. An operator or hardware configuration intending to boot in bare-metal (restricted) mode will always receive the Linux boot image instead. This could allow privilege escalation or bypass of intended boot-time security policies enforced by the bare-metal boot ROM. Conversely, there is no way to enforce bare-metal-only boot through this hardware control.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The definition and content of `rom_rdata_bm` and the bare-metal boot ROM instantiation are not shown in the provided snippets, but the declared signal name and the broken mux assignment provide sufficient evidence for the finding. It is possible `rom_rdata_bm` is also wired to `rom_rdata_linux` elsewhere, but the mux itself is definitively broken as shown.

finding.summary: AXI protection field (prot) for RSA peripheral is hardcoded to zero, disabling AXI privilege, security, and instruction access protection on all transactions

evidence.description: Both rsa_axi_req.aw.prot and rsa_axi_req.ar.prot are unconditionally assigned '0 (line 1077 and 1088). The AXI PROT field encodes three attributes: privileged/unprivileged access, secure/non-secure access, and data/instruction access. Forcing all three bits to zero means every transaction to the RSA peripheral is marked as unprivileged, secure, and data — with no ability to enforce security-level checking at the interconnect or downstream slave.
evidence.supports_claim: The prot signal is hardwired to zero with no guard, meaning the RSA block cannot differentiate between secure and non-secure masters or privileged and unprivileged requestors. An unprivileged or non-secure bus master can access the RSA peripheral without any AXI-level protection check firing.

evidence.description: The rsa_axi_req structure drives an RSA cryptographic accelerator peripheral over AXI. RSA key material and operation registers are sensitive resources that should only be accessible by privileged, secure bus masters.
evidence.supports_claim: RSA accelerator registers are high-value targets; unprotected AXI PROT bits remove a layer of bus-fabric access control.

evidence.description: The comment 'tie off signals not used by AXI-lite' at line 1070 indicates the developer intentionally zeroed these fields as part of an AXI-to-AXI-lite adaptation, but the PROT field is behaviorally significant even in AXI-lite contexts and should reflect the actual privilege/security level of the initiating master.
evidence.supports_claim: There is no guard logic; the choice to zero PROT was an explicit design decision rather than an omission, yet it removes AXI protection semantics.

finding.reasoning_summary: The source snippet shows that rsa_axi_req.aw.prot and rsa_axi_req.ar.prot are both hardcoded to zero via unconditional continuous assignments. The AXI PROT field (3 bits) controls whether a transaction is privileged, secure, and whether it is a data or instruction fetch. Hardcoding to zero eliminates the ability of any downstream AXI interconnect or slave to gate access based on bus-master privilege or security level. For a cryptographic accelerator like RSA, this is a concrete permission bypass: any master on the bus, regardless of its actual privilege or security state, will present identical PROT bits to the RSA slave.
finding.security_impact: Any AXI master, including unprivileged or non-secure software agents, can issue read and write transactions to the RSA peripheral that are indistinguishable (at the AXI PROT level) from privileged secure accesses. This defeats bus-fabric access controls and could allow unauthorized access to RSA key registers or operation results, enabling cryptographic key extraction or misuse of the RSA engine.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The actual impact depends on whether the downstream RSA slave or AXI interconnect enforces PROT-based filtering. If the interconnect ignores PROT (common in simple designs), this finding has lower practical impact. Additionally, higher-level software or SoC-level memory protection units (MPU/IOPMP) may compensate. The snippet does not show how the RSA slave or the interconnect uses the PROT field.
